name: Chalchiuhtlicue
description: Chalchiuhtlicue, whose name translates to "She of the Jade Skirt," is a prominent Aztec goddess associated with water, rivers, seas, streams, storms, and baptism. She is considered the patroness of childbirth and is also linked to the purification of the world through water. In Aztec cosmology, she ruled over the Fourth Sun, Nahui-Atl (Four Water), which was destroyed by a great flood, turning humans into fish. She is often depicted wearing a green skirt, symbolizing precious jade and the life-giving properties of water. As a fertility goddess, Chalchiuhtlicue is revered for her ability to nurture and sustain life. Sacrifices, often involving aquatic creatures or precious objects, were offered to her to ensure the continuation of rainfall and the health of water sources. Her influence extended beyond physical sustenance, encompassing emotional and spiritual well-being, as water was seen as a purifier of the soul. She is a vital figure in Aztec beliefs, representing the powerful and essential role of water in their world.
subtitle: Goddess of Water, Rivers, and Seas

keyMyths.title: The Fourth Sun
keyMyths.description: Chalchiuhtlicue ruled the Fourth Sun, Nahui-Atl, which ended in a catastrophic flood. This flood transformed humans into fish, marking the end of that era.

keyMyths.title: Goddess of Fresh Water
keyMyths.description: Chalchiuhtlicue is the goddess of fresh water, providing sustenance to the Aztec people and their crops. She is responsible for the rivers, lakes, and springs that sustained life.

keyMyths.title: Patroness of Childbirth
keyMyths.description: As the patroness of childbirth, women prayed to Chalchiuhtlicue for a safe delivery and healthy children. Her connection to water symbolized the amniotic fluid and the life-giving force.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
